purpose: Defines the PRIMARY swarm command `!p_track [--dashboard {id}] {prompt}` — full parallel swarm with dependency-aware dispatch and live dashboard tracking. The invoking agent becomes the master orchestrator and runs three phases: Phase 1 Planning (deep analysis, write plan.json with deep-thinking output, then initialization.json+logs.json, present plan, await approval), Phase 2 Execution (dispatch with eager dependency-driven scan after every event, full self-contained worker prompts, circuit breaker on failures), Phase 3 Completion (verify, write metrics.json, deliver NON-NEGOTIABLE final report, save history). Output: dashboards/{dashboardId}/plan.json (canonical), initialization.json, logs.json, progress/{task_id}.json (workers), master_state.json (master), metrics.json.
gotchas: p_track requires plan.json BEFORE initialization.json — validate-plan-required.sh hook will block initialization.json writes when plan.json is missing/invalid
agents.length in initialization.json MUST equal plan.tasks.length — hook enforced
Dispatch FIRST, update tracker AFTER — the agent must be launched before logs.json is updated; this is the single most important dispatch rule
Dependency-driven dispatch, NOT wave-driven — waves are visual only; dispatch logic looks ONLY at individual task depends_on
Full Dashboard Tracking is MANDATORY at 3+ parallel agents OR >1 wave — multi-wave is non-negotiable
started_at and completed_at for the overall swarm are NO LONGER written by the master to initialization.json — derived client-side from worker progress files
If 3+ tasks fail in same wave, OR a failure blocks >50% remaining, circuit breaker triggers — pause and reassess with user (Step 15G)
Workers own ALL lifecycle data (status, started_at, completed_at, summary, milestones, deviations, logs) via progress files — master never writes these
Cancellation is THE one exception where master writes progress files
If deep-thinking context is forgotten (e.g., after compaction), redo the analysis until each task's `approach` clearly fulfills the original prompt
Permission requests: write `permission` level log entry to logs.json FIRST (triggers dashboard popup), THEN ask in terminal
Master MUST NOT write code — every implementation, file edit, code change goes through worker agents via Task tool
Final report is NON-NEGOTIABLE — read all progress files, logs, master task; deliver complete written report covering summary/files/deviations/improvements/future-steps every time
patterns: Three phases: Phase 1 Planning (Steps 1-11) → Phase 2 Execution (Steps 12-16) → Phase 3 Completion (Step 17)
Phase 1 detail deferred to agent/_commands/p_track_planning.md; Phase 2 to p_track_execution.md; Phase 3 to p_track_completion.md
Shared File Decision Tree: separate files (Pattern C) > integration task (Pattern B) > owner task (Pattern A)
Right-sizing: 1-5 minutes per task, 1-2 files modified, 2-3 files read
Atomic write protocol: read → modify in memory → write the full file; never write partial JSON
Convention map filtering: master extracts CLAUDE.md sections per worker (per Convention Relevance Checklist in worker_prompts.md); workers don't re-read
Upstream result cache: every completion's summary/files/exports/deviations stored in master memory; injected into downstream prompts' UPSTREAM RESULTS section
Eager dispatch loop: every event (completion/failure) → scan all pending tasks → dispatch ALL whose deps are now complete → in same cycle
conventions: Top-of-file NON-NEGOTIABLE RULES blockquote (5 numbered rules — no code, must read instructions, must use dashboard, must dispatch via workers, must deliver final report)
Output Files block listing the canonical artifacts and their hook enforcement
Final Rules section with 31 numbered rules grouped by domain (Dispatch+Tracking, Statusing, Agent Prompts, Upstream+Caching, Planning, Parallelization, Verification+Reporting, Permission Requests)
Embedded Shared File Decision Tree as ASCII flowchart
